coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent and morphology-controllable graphene quantum dots-chitosan hybrid xerogels for in vivo imaging and pH-sensitive drug carrier
coredata.dc:creator: Lv, Ouyang
Tao, Yongxin
Qin, Yong
Chen, Chuanxiang
Pan, Yan
Deng, Linhong
Liu, Li
Kong, Yong
coredata.dc:description: Highly fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-chitosan (CS) hybrid xerogels (GQDs-CS) were facilely synthesized, and the morphology of GQDs-CS was controllable by varying the content of GQDs in the xerogel. The GQDs-CS exhibited a porous and three-dimensional (3D) network structure when the content of GQDs reached 43% (wt%) in the xerogel, which was beneficial for drug loading and sustained release. The as-prepared GQDs-CS could also be applied for in vivo imaging since it showed strong blue, green and red luminescence under excitation of varying wavelengths. Moreover, the pH-induced protonation/deprotonation of the –NH2 groups on CS chains can result in a pH-dependent drug delivery behavior of the GQDs-CS hybrid xerogel.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Chitosan
Xerogel
In vivo imagine
Drug delivery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro and in vivo fluorescence imaging
Release of SS from GQDs-CS xerogel
FT-IR spectra of CS, GQDs and GQDs-CS xerogels
Morphology and size of CS, GQDs and GQDs-CS xerogels
In vivo fluorescence imaging using GQDs-CS xerogel
pH-Sensitive in vitro drug release from GQDs-CS xerogels
Cumulative release of SS from GQDs-CS xerogels with different GQDs contents

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration showing the formation of the drug-loaded GQDs-CS hybrid xerogel.
Determined zeta potentials of CS and GQDs.
Digital photographs of the mixtures composed of different proportions of CS and GQDs and the formed hybrid xerogels.
FT-IR spectra of CS, GQDs and GQDs-CS hybrid xerogels with different GQDs contents.
(A) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms at 77K and (B) pore-size distribution of GQDs-CS (GQDs, 43wt%).
FESEM and TEM images of CS xerogel (A), GQDs-CS (GQDs, 25wt%) (B), GQDs-CS (GQDs, 30wt%) (C), GQDs-CS (GQDs, 43wt%) (D) and GQDs (E).

Laser scanning confocal fluorescence images of CS (A), GQDs-CS (GQDs, 25wt%) (B), GQDs-CS (GQDs, 30wt%) (C), GQDs-CS (GQDs, 43wt%) (D) and GQDs (E) under different excitation wavelengths of 405, 430 and 535nm.

(A) Real-time in vivo fluorescence imaging of a nude mouse after subcutaneous injection of GQDs-CS xerogel (GQDs, 43wt%) (right) and PBS blank control (left). (B) In vivo fluorescence imaging after subcutaneous injection of GQDs-CS xerogel (right) and PBS blank control (left) for 5h. (C) In vivo imaging by injection of GQDs (right) and PBS blank control (left). (D) In vivo imaging by injection of GQDs (right) and PBS blank control (left) for 5h.
pH-Dependent release of SS from GQDs-CS (GQDs, 43wt%) (A) and GQDs-CS (GQDs, 25wt%) (B) under different pH values (4.5, 7.4, and 8.5) at 37°C.
Chemical structures of sodium salicylate (A) and doxorubicin (B).
In vitro release of SS from pure CS xerogel (a), GQDs-CS (GQDs, 30wt%) (b), GQDs-CS (GQDs, 25wt%) (c) and GQDs-CS (GQDs, 43wt%) (d) in 0.1M PBS of pH7.4 at 37°C.

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent and morphology-controllable graphene quantum dots-chitosan hybrid xerogels for in vivo imaging and pH-sensitive drug carrier

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center, School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering
Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Health Sciences, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Health Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of pharmaceutical Engineering & Life Science, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of pharmaceutical Engineering & Life Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-chitosan (CS) hybrid xerogels (GQDs-CS) were facilely synthesized, and the morphology of GQDs-CS was controllable by varying the content of GQDs in the xerogel. The GQDs-CS exhibited a porous and three-dimensional (3D) network structure when the content of GQDs reached 43% (wt%) in the xerogel, which was beneficial for drug loading and sustained release. The as-prepared GQDs-CS could also be applied for in vivo imaging since it showed strong blue, green and red luminescence under excitation of varying wavelengths. Moreover, the pH-induced protonation/deprotonation of the –NH2 groups on CS chains can result in a pH-dependent drug delivery behavior of the GQDs-CS hybrid xerogel.

Highly fluorescent and morphology-controllable graphene quantum dots-chitosan hybrid xerogels for in vivo imaging and pH-sensitive drug carrier.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: The past years have witnessed the development of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) because of their strong quantum confinement and edge effects [1,2]. However, tremendous efforts were devoted to developing approaches for GQDs synthesis (bottom-up and top-down methods) and exploring their properties [3–5], and their applications in biomedical field have not been extensively explored. Recently, GQDs have become the focus for fluorescent probes because GQDs can emit fluorescence of various wavelengths including near-infrared fluorescence (NIR) [6]. The size- and composition-tunable luminescence, high photostability and low toxicity make GQDs a promising material for drug carrier and in vivo imaging [7–12].

Chitosan (CS) is a natural cationic biopolymer composed of 2-amino-2-deoxy(1.4)-β-d-glucopyranose units, which has been widely used in biomedical and tissue engineering field due to its good biocompatibility, biodegradability, non-toxicity and antibacterial properties [13–16]. Of particular interest, CS exhibits intriguing pH-responsive swelling/deswelling transitions induced by protonation and deprotonation of the –NH2 groups at the interpenetrated CS chains [17,18], and thus CS based pH-regulated drug delivery has been realized [19–21].

In the current study, CS was incorporated into GQDs through electrostatic interactions and H-bonding, and then GQDs-CS xerogel was prepared via a simple freeze-drying technique. The influence of GQDs content on the microstructure and fluorescent property of the xerogel was fully addressed. The feasibility of using the GQDs-CS as a fluorescent biomarker was investigated through in vitro and in vivo fluorescence imaging through subcutaneous injection in an animal model. Finally, the GQDs-CS was applied as a drug carrier, and it exhibited pH-sensitive drug delivery behavior, which opens a new avenue in the design of pH-triggered delivery at specific sites and in the protection of drugs from degradation when passing through different tissues and organs in the human body [22]. Here sodium salicylate (SS) is chosen as the model drug because it is widely used as prescribed agents for the treatment of inflammation and for curing diseases including wound healing [23], diabetes [24], arthritis [25] and cancer treatment [26].

Citric acid monohydrate, chitosan (with a deacetylation degree of 85%), sodium salicylate (SS) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. BALB/c nude mice were supplied by Department of Experimental Animals, Fudan University (China). All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2MΩ.

The zeta potentials of GQDs and CS were determined by a ZEN3600 zeta potential analyzer (Malvern). The morphology and size of GQDs, CS and GQDs-CS with different GQDs contents were observed on a Supra55 field-emission scanning electron microscope (Zeiss, Germany) and a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan), respectively. The FT-IR spectra of different samples were measured by a FTIR-8400S spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms were recorded at 77K with an ASAP 2010 specific surface area and pore size analyzer (Micromeritics, USA). The in vitro fluorescence imaging of GQDs-CS and GQDs was conducted at different excitation wavelengths (Zeiss LSM-710 Confocal Microsope), and the in vivo fluorescence imaging of nude mice with subcutaneously injected GQDs-CS was carried out by an IVIS imaging system (Caliper).

GQDs were prepared according to the method previously reported [27]. In a typical synthesis, 2g citric acid monohydrate was put into a ceramic crucible and heated to 200°C in a muffle furnace. After 30min, the muffle furnace was gradually cooled down to room temperature and brown GQDs powders were obtained. Next, 20mg GQDs were dispersed in 10mL ultrapure water by sonification to obtain a 2mgmL−1 yellow GQDs dispersion.

Different amounts of CS powders were dissolved in 10mL 0.1M acetic acid solutions, and then the CS solutions of different concentrations (2, 4 and 6mgmL−1) were respectively transferred into the GQDs dispersion (2mgmL−1). The mixtures were vigorously stirred for 10min, followed by neutralizing with diluted NaOH solution, and yellow solid (GQDs-CS) was precipitated. The products were separated and washed with ultrapure water several times to remove unreacted GQDs and CS, and subsequently freeze-dried in a lyophilizer under −45°C for 24h to obtain the GQDs-CS hybrid xerogel. For control experiment, pure CS xerogel was prepared by the same procedure without adding GQDs.

Each xerogel sample with the same mass (100mg) was separately dispersed in 100mL ultrapure water under stirring, and then 50mg SS was added into the hybrid xerogel dispersion. After a continuous stirring for 24h in the dark (SS is stable in the dark), the drug-loaded hybrids were then separated from the dispersion by filtering, washed thoroughly to remove unloaded drug, and freeze-dried to yield drug-loaded GQDs-CS xerogel. The schematic illustration showing the formation of the drug-loaded hybrid xerogel is shown in Fig. 1 . The drug loading capacity and entrapment efficiency were calculated by the following two equations.
Drug loading capacity (%)=100%×(weight of drug in the substrate)/(weight of the substrate) [28] Entrapment efficiency (%)=100%×(weight of drug in the substrate)/(weight of the feeding drug) [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo fluorescence imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The in vitro fluorescence imaging of 1mgmL−1 dispersion of CS, GQDs and GQDs-CS with various contents GQDs was conducted at different excitation wavelengths of 405, 430 and 535nm. For in vivo fluorescence imaging, BALB/c nude mice, aging 4–5weeks and weighing 20±2g, were anesthetized and subcutaneously injected with 100μL aliquot of 5mgmL−1 GQDs-CS (GQDs, 43wt%) PBS solution (pH7.4). For control experiments, another group of nude mice were subcutaneously injected with 100μL PBS solution without GQDs-CS. All animal experiments were performed in compliance with the institutional animal use and care regulations approved by the ethics committee of Fudan University. The fluorescence images were immediately taken under excitation wavelength at 535nm and emission wavelength at 740nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Release of SS from GQDs-CS xerogel
ce:section.ce:para: The pH-sensitive release of SS from the hybrid xerogels was tested by dialysis approach. 15mg drug-loaded hybrid xerogels were dispersed in 5mL 0.1M PBS solutions of different pH values (4.5, 7.4 and 8.5), and subsequently filled into three dialysis bags (molecular weight cut-off, 3500), respectively. Next, the three dialysis bags filled with 5mL diluted drug-loaded GQDs-CS were immersed into 45mL 0.1M PBS solutions with the corresponding pHs (4.5, 7.4 and 8.5) at 37°C under stirring. The released SS was sampled (3mL) at defined time intervals and assayed by UV–vis spectrometry at 296nm according to a predetermined calibration curve, and an equal volume of fresh PBS was used to replenish it. Cumulative release was expressed as the total percentage of drug released at time t versus the initial loaded drug amount [30].

The surface charge of CS and GQDs was determined by measuring their zeta potentials, and the results are shown in Fig. 2 . CS (pKa ≈6.2 ) exhibits a zeta potential value of +65.3mV in 0.1M acetic acid (pH4.0), which is due to the protonation of amine groups (−NH2) in its repeating units [31,32]. However, the zeta potential value of GQDs is found to be −9.6mV attributed to the ionization of oxygen-containing groups on the surface of GQDs (–COOH, –OH and epoxide functional groups). As a result, it is expected that the highly positively charged CS will complex with the negatively charged GQDs through the electrostatic attractions. On the other hand, the H-bonding associations among the –OH/–COOH groups of GQDs and the –NH2/–OH groups of CS are also beneficial for the formation of the GQDs-CS hybrid xerogel [33].

It is noteworthy that three different concentrations of CS solutions (2, 4 and 6mgmL−1) are tested for the interactions with 2mgmL−1 GQDs dispersion, and the digital photographs of the formed mixtures and the corresponding hybrid xerogels are shown in Fig. 3 . GQDs or CS is excessive in the obtained mixtures when the CS concentration is below 2mgmL−1 or above 6mgmL−1. As a result, the CS concentrations used here are set between 2 and 6mgmL−1, and the contents of GQDs in the xerogels after freeze-drying are calculated to be 43%, 30% and 25%, respectively, corresponding to the CS concentrations of 2, 4 and 6mgmL−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR spectra of CS, GQDs and GQDs-CS xerogels
ce:section.ce:para: The FT-IR spectra are investigated to understand the interactions between GQDs and CS. As shown in Fig. 4 , the characteristic peaks of CS appearing at 1650 and 1604cm−1 are attributable to the CO stretching vibrations of NHCO and the NH bending vibrations of NH2, respectively [34]. In the spectrum of GQDs, the peaks at 1766 and 1716cm−1 are characteristics of the antisymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of CO of the COOH groups [35]. Compared with pure CS and GQDs, the peaks at 1766 and 1604cm−1 disappear completely in the spectra of GQDs-CS xerogels with different GQDs contents; meanwhile, the characteristic peak at 1716cm−1 is red-shifted to 1704cm−1. The changes in the spectra of the GQDs-CS xerogels can be attributed to the H-bonding associations between GQDs and CS.

Porosity analysis of the as-prepared GQDs-CS xerogel (GQDs, 43wt%) is carried out by nitrogen isothermal adsorption-desorption experiments at 77K. As shown in Fig. 5A, the profile is characterized by an irregular Type I isotherm since an appreciable rise on the isotherm at the relative pressure of 0.8 is observed. As previously reviewed [36], a classical Type I isotherm exhibits prominent adsorption below the relative pressure of about 0.1 and then level off for the materials to be exclusively microporous (below 2nm). When a Type I isotherm does not level off at low relative pressures, the sample is likely to exhibit a pronounced amount of mesopores or even be exclusively mesoporous (between 2 and 50nm), which is further verified by the pore-size distribution of the hybrid xerogel (Fig. 5B). As can be seen, the total pore volume of the GQDs-CS (GQDs, 43wt%) is 0.033cm3 g−1 with the pore-size distribution of 4.39nm measured by the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method, demonstrating that the as-prepared GQDs-CS xerogel is a mesoporous material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and size of CS, GQDs and GQDs-CS xerogels
ce:section.ce:para: Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) are employed to investigate the surface morphologies and size distributions of the hybrid xerogels with different amounts of GQDs (Fig. 6 ). As can be seen, pure CS exhibits a flake-like structure, and serious agglomeration is observed due to the interactions among the protonated CS chains [37]. After GQDs are introduced into the hybrid xerogels, spherical GQDs with an average diameter of ~4.8nm are evenly distributed within the CS matrix, resulting from the strong anchoring effect between CS and GQDs. More interestingly, it is noted that with the increase in the GQDs content (from 25wt% to 43wt%), the distribution of GQDs within the CS matrix becomes more densely and the structure of the obtained GQDs-CS xerogel gradually turns to be a porous three-dimensional (3D) network, which is particularly beneficial for drug loading and sustained release. By comparing the size distribution histograms of GQDs-CS and GQDs (insets of Fig. 6D and E), it is also found that the addition of CS does not alter the size distributions of GQDs in the hybrid xerogels.

To explore the fluorescence features of the developed GQDs-CS xerogels, the confocal fluorescence images of the hybrid xerogels are recorded upon irradiation with different wavelengths (Fig. 7 ). For pure CS, no fluorescence emission is found under different excitation wavelengths, however, the emitted fluorescence from the GQDs-CS xerogels is clearly observed after the introduction of GQDs, indicating that GQDs have been successfully combined with CS via electrostatic attractions and H-bondings. The fluorescence emission exhibits an excitation-dependent manner, i.e., the hybrid xerogels emit blue, green and red fluorescence when the excitation wavelength is fixed at 405, 430 and 535nm, respectively. On the other hand, the fluorescence intensity is increased with increasing content of GQDs in the hybrid xerogels, and the strongest fluorescence is obtained from the GQDs-CS xerogel (GQDs, 43wt%), demonstrating that this type of GQDs-CS xerogel is most suitable for in vivo fluorescence imaging. Also, it should be pointed out that the emitted fluorescence from pure GQDs is significantly weaker than that from the GQDs-CS xerogel (GQDs, 43wt%) especially under the excitation wavelength of 535nm, which might be attributed to the inner filter effect resulted from extremely high concentration of GQDs [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo fluorescence imaging using GQDs-CS xerogel
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the GQDs-CS xerogel for in vivo fluorescence imaging in nude mice was investigated. 100μL aliquot of 5mgmL−1 GQDs-CS (GQDs, 43wt%) was injected subcutaneously into two spots near the left fore leg of a nude mouse, and controlled experiments are carried out by injecting PBS without GQDs-CS. Fig. 8 shows the real-time in vivo fluorescence bioimaging. Under the excitation wavelength of 535nm, strong red fluorescence (emission wavelength, 740nm) can be clearly observed at the two spots where GQDs-CS xerogel is injected, however, no emission is observed at the spots where only PBS is injected (Fig. 8A). After a duration of 5h, the red fluorescence can still be observed just at the two injected spots without obvious spread (Fig. 8B). This phenomenon might be attributed to the macromolecular chains of CS in the xerogel, which prevents the GQDs-CS xerogel from free spreading in the body of the mouse. On the contrary, the red fluorescence at the two spots disappears almost completely after 5h by injecting PBS with only GQDs (Fig. 8C versus 8D), and the similar phenomena regarding free quantum dots have also been reported by Zheng et al. [39]. It can be concluded that the stable red fluorescence in the nude mouse is attributed to the existence of CS polymers in the hybrid xerogels. The results demonstrate that the GQDs-CS hybrid xerogel can potentially use as an excellent labeling material for in vivo imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-Sensitive in vitro drug release from GQDs-CS xerogels
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9A shows the cumulative releasing profiles of SS from the GQDs-CS xerogel (GQDs, 43wt%) obtained at different pH values of 4.5, 7.4, and 8.5, respectively. As can be seen, the first 100min shows the fastest release rate for all the three pH values. After that, the release rate slows down for each pH. The release percentages of SS from the hybrid xerogel are 61%, 74% and 85% at the pH of 4.5, 7.4 and 8.5, respectively, at 37°C after 420min. It is interesting to find that the pH dependency of the SS releasing profiles from the GQDs-CS (GQDs, 43wt%) is opposite to the release of doxorubicin (DOX) from CS-based nanocomposites [19,30]. i.e., the release percentage of DOX decreases with increasing pH value. To explain the two opposite pH-dependent drug release behaviors, another GQDs-CS xerogel (GQDs, 25wt%) is applied for the in vitro release of SS at different pH values. As can be seen from Fig. 9B, the drug releasing profiles from the GQDs-CS (GQDs, 25wt%) at different pH values are similar to those from GQDs-CS (GQDs, 43wt%). That is to say, the release percentage of SS from the GQDs-CS xerogels increases with increasing pH value, no matter GQDs or CS is excessive in the xerogels.

From the above comparison, it is clear that not the nature of the drug carrier, but the interactions between the drug and the carrier play a crucial role in the pH-dependent release of the loaded drug. The chemical structures of DOX and SS are shown in Fig. 10 . The decrease in pH enables the protonation of NH2 groups on the DOX molecules (pKa ≈8.2) [40] and the CS chains, resulting in electrostatic repulsions between the positively charged DOX and CS chains in acidic solutions. Therefore, the release percentage of DOX is much higher under acidic conditions than under neutral and alkaline conditions. In contrary, SS is negatively charged at pH4.5 since the pKa of acidic SS is 3.0 [41]. At pH4.5, the electrostatic attractions between SS and positively charged CS (pKa ≈6.2 ) hinder the release of SS from the GQDs-CS xerogels. With the increase in pH from 4.5 to 8.5, the CS in the xerogels is gradually deprotonated, resulting in weakened electrostatic attractions and facilitated delivery of SS from the GQDs-CS xerogels. A similar pH dependency of the SS releasing profiles from the CS micro/nanoparticles has also been reported previously [42]. The pH-sensitive drug delivery behavior is of great importance for an intelligent and smart drug carrier because many pathological processes in tumor tissues and intracellular lysosomes and endosomes are accompanied with a local pH change compared with that in normal tissues [43,44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cumulative release of SS from GQDs-CS xerogels with different GQDs contents
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 11 compares the SS releasing profiles from pure CS xerogel and the GQDs-CS xerogels with different GQDs contents at 37°C at certain time points (pH7.4). As can be seen, the GQDs-CS xerogel (GQDs, 43wt%) exhibits the fastest release rate and highest cumulative release amount of SS (1.36mg). The superior drug delivery behavior of GQDs-CS (GQDs, 43wt%) can be attributed to its unique porous 3D network structure, which is different from pure CS xerogel and other GQDs-CS xerogels (GQDs, 25wt% and 30wt%), as shown in Fig. 6. Finally, the drug loading capacity and the entrapment efficiency of the GQDs-CS xerogel (GQDs, 43wt%) are calculated to be 13% and 30%, respectively, by quantitative analysis.

In summary, highly fluorescent and morphology-controllable GQDs-CS hybrid xerogels are prepared through electrostatic attractions and H-bonding associations between CS and GQDs. The as-made xerogels exhibit various morphologies with different GQDs contents. The GQDs-CS xerogel (GQDs, 43wt%) possesses a porous 3D network structure and emits fluorescence of the highest intensity under various excitation wavelengths, showing the potential to be used for in vitro and in vivo fluorescence imaging. Moreover, the release of SS is pH-sensitive, and the GQDs-CS xerogels exhibit superior SS release under alkaline conditions compared with that under acidic and neutral conditions, which is contrary to the release of DOX from the CS-based nanocomposites. This is mainly attributed to the opposite electrostatic interactions between the drug molecules (SS or DOX) and the CS-based carrier resulted from the pH-induced protonation/deprotonation of the CS chains. Due to its unique porous 3D network structure, the GQDs-CS xerogel (GQDs, 43wt%) loaded with the drug SS seems to be a promising carrier material for use as implantable and pH-triggered drug delivery system, which might open up new opportunities for in vivo medical diagnostics and effective therapy.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21275023, 11532003) and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).
